title: The Year After You Spoke Up
dek: You finally said the thing you were never supposed to say. Now comes the long, quiet, and deeply physical work of letting your body adjust to the shape of its own truth.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Immediate Aftermath of Speaking Up


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T01:52:43.184Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

It’s 3 AM. You lie awake, the ceiling fan’s slow, uneven hum pressing against the tightness knotting deep in your jaw. The masseter muscle - usually a quiet, background player in the mundane act of chewing - feels like it’s clenched shut on a bite of gravel. The taste of those words you finally said is sour in your mouth, bitter as unripe fruit. The adrenaline that surged through your veins just hours before has slipped away, like a tide pulling out to sea, leaving behind a raw, aching emptiness where your courage briefly lived.

Speaking up was a body event, not just a mental victory or a moral statement. When the words left your mouth, they didn’t just float free in the air; they traveled through the sinewy, stubborn tissues of your face and throat. The masseter muscle tightened, the levator scapulae at the back of your neck locked into a rigid brace, and your diaphragm fluttered with a wild, jagged rhythm. This wasn’t just “talking.” It was a storm in your body’s landscape, a seismic shift beneath your skin.

You can still feel the way your jaw remembers the moment. That hard clench, like a fist made of muscle and fascia, holding the unyielding core of your truth. The masseter, often overlooked, is a clenched gatekeeper here. It carries the weight of your fury and your fear in equal measure. When you finally spoke, it tightened so fiercely that the skin above it felt stretched thin, almost translucent. That muscle doesn’t forget. Neither does your temporal bone, where the tendons anchor tight, as if bracing for something you didn’t fully know how to name.

And then came the crash. The adrenaline that had fueled your voice, that had made your words sharp and your stance unshakable, vanished with the suddenness of a storm’s calm center. Your body, once wired for fight or flight, now sinks into exhaustion. The trapezius muscles - those broad, burden-bearing sheets across your shoulders - start to ache from holding tension you didn’t even realize you were carrying. Your chest feels heavy, as if the diaphragm, that dome-shaped muscle beneath your ribs, is stubbornly resisting its usual smooth, rhythmic dance.

Cold sweat beads at your temples, dripping slow and insistent, a reminder that your body is still processing the upheaval. This is not just mental fatigue; it’s a visceral, unrelenting somatic truth. Your skin smells faintly of stress - an iron-scented musk that clings to the sheets like a second skin. You’re not just tired. You’re undone.

The hours stretch in the dark. Your body replays the moment in microseconds, every twitch and every tightening an echo. The masseter remembers, the levator scapulae remembers, the diaphragm remembers. This is not pathology, this is biology. Your body is telling you what your mind can’t yet fully grasp: that speaking up is a radical act of embodiment. It shakes your whole system loose and asks for repair.

Teachers like Robert Augustus Masters remind us that this is part of the process - this unspooling of tension after the storm. The body does not simply snap back to baseline after such a rupture. It takes time, and it takes attention. The 3 AM cold sweat, the jaw that won’t unclench, are the body’s way of saying, “We’re still here. We’re still holding.” There is no shame in this. The body knows.

You may reach for herbal allies in these moments - something to ease the tightness in your jaw, to soothe the overworked diaphragm, to calm the tempest in your nervous system. A cup of skullcap tea, steeped long and slow, can cradle your trembling muscles. Licorice root, with its gentle sweetness, supports the adrenal glands that have been wrung dry. This is serious medicine, not fluff, and it honors the body’s wisdom as it moves through the aftermath.

The night after you spoke up is not a quiet one. It’s a reckoning. Your body, fierce and unyielding, demands to be heard just as much as your words did. The masseter muscle clenches, the sweat gathers, and the diaphragm flutters. This is the realness of courage - the cost, the cost, the deep somatic truth that no one warns you about but everyone carries.

## The First Three Months: The Silence That Follows

You speak up. Maybe it’s a tremulous thing, a barely-there crack in your usual silence, or maybe it’s a roar that shakes the walls of your life. Either way, once the words spill out, the world tilts beneath your feet. What happens next is not the relief you’d hoped for. Instead, there’s a silence, thick and heavy, that descends like a storm cloud refusing to break. Your body knows this moment - because it’s waiting, bracing, holding its breath.

In those first three months, your psoas muscle - the deep, central muscle that hugs your lumbar spine and cradles your pelvis - locks down like a fortress. This is the muscle that remembers your survival, the one that tenses before you even fully comprehend the fallout. It’s the body’s primal anchor, tightening as if to prepare you for battle or flight, even when your mind is still reeling, trying to understand what you just unleashed. You’ll notice it if you pay attention: a dull ache deep in your lower back, a subtle stiffness when you shift from sitting to standing, a gnawing tension that feels like your core is holding your very soul in place.

This muscle doesn’t lie. It’s bracing for impact because the ripples from your words are still spreading, and sometimes, the echoes come back in the form of cold silences, sideways glances, or the sudden absence of people who once felt like your safe ground. The relationships you thought would hold steady begin to shift, like the slow, inexorable movement of tectonic plates. You might feel it first in your shoulders - the trapezius and levator scapulae tightening, drawing your head slightly forward and down, a somatic bowing away from the unexpected distance forming between you and others.

Karla McLaren, whose work on anger has been a lighthouse for many of us navigating these turbulent waters, calls anger a boundary-restoring force. Her insight cuts through the common cultural narrative that frames anger as something to be tamed or feared. Instead, she teaches us that anger is the body’s way of saying, “This is not okay. This is not safe.” When you spoke up, you sent a signal to your body and to those around you that your boundaries are real, that your needs matter. But boundaries unsettle. They make others uncomfortable, especially if they’ve been accustomed to your silence or compliance. That discomfort can manifest as withdrawal, avoidance, or even quiet retaliation.

The silence that follows your declaration is not emptiness. It vibrates with unspoken tension - like the charged stillness before a thunderclap. Your body feels this tension in the subtle tightening along your sternum, the slight constriction of your diaphragm, as if your breath itself is cautious now, measuring every inhale and exhale. Nights feel longer. Maybe around 3 a.m., when the world is hushed and the air smells faintly of damp earth and cold stone, you find yourself awake, your body replaying conversations, recalibrating its sense of safety. The masseter muscle clenches unconsciously as your jaw remembers the words you held back before, the ones you finally let out. You might taste the metallic tang of adrenaline still lingering, a reminder that your nervous system remains on alert.

This is not pathology. This is biology. Your body is doing what it’s wired to do: protect you. The psoas, the diaphragm, the trapezius - each carries a map of your survival story, including this newest chapter of boundary-setting and the fallout that follows. The shifting relationships, the silences, the ruptures - they are all part of your body’s ongoing conversation with the world. And while this period is brutal in its uncertainty, it is also the crucible where your true self begins to emerge, raw and unvarnished.

You might find solace in herbs that speak to this fierce, bracing energy. Something like [Gaia Herbs SleepThru](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005H1ENTQ?tag=spankyspinola-20) or [Gaia Herbs PRO Calm Restore](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0BTMTSSZ6?tag=spankyspinola-20) can gently ease the diaphragm’s tight grasp and coax the psoas muscle toward release. But more than anything, you need to listen - to the body’s subtle cues, to the shifting rhythms of your breath, to the slow unwinding of tension in the deep muscle fibers that carry your story. This is where healing begins - not in the noise of others’ reactions, but in the quiet reclamation of your own body’s wisdom.

## The Six-Month Mark: The Weight of New Boundaries

Six months after you finally spoke your truth, the exhausted ache in your trapezius and levator scapulae isn’t just muscle fatigue; it’s a ledger of other people’s disappointment settling into your body like sediment. You notice it first in the early morning, just as dawn softens the night’s chill, when your neck feels tight as if invisible hands are threading a noose of tension around your cervical spine. That mass of fascia between your shoulders - taut, unyielding - carries the weight of the boundaries you set, the ones they never asked for and didn’t want.

This isn’t a story about weakness. The trapezius, that triangular muscle spanning from the base of your skull to the middle of your back, is the body’s natural shrug. But yours has been holding a prolonged, stubborn shrug, as if bracing against an invisible storm of unmet expectations and quiet resentments. Alongside it, the levator scapulae - those small muscles nesting at the back and side of your neck - grip your scapulae like a vise, their chronic contraction a somatic echo of the emotional labor you didn’t bargain for.

You carry the weight of their disappointment as if it were your own failing. But this is not pathology. This is biology, the body’s way of recording the psychic strain of new boundaries, the unspoken words, the cold shoulders, the recalibrations that come with claiming your space. Resmaa Menakem’s teachings come to mind here - the way the trauma of others can imprint onto your tissues, how the body remembers collective pain and personal rupture alike. Your body remembers, even if your mind wants to forget.

The evenings are the hardest. Around 9 PM, when the world quiets down and your nervous system should be settling into repair, your neck and shoulders rebel. The muscles knot, the fascia thickens, and a dull throb settles behind your ears. It smells like your skin is holding onto the day’s stress - a faint, sour tang of sweat mingled with the faint metallic aroma of tension. You find yourself reaching for the balm of herbs long trusted by women who knew the cost of holding space for others while trying to hold themselves together.

Nervines like skullcap and milky oats become your allies in this slow, somatic unraveling. Skullcap (Scutellaria lateriflora) is a gentle yet potent herb, its leaves and flowers steeped into deep, dark tea that tastes like the earth after rain. It soothes the nervous system, coaxing the trapezius and levator scapulae to release their grip, inviting the muscles to soften rather than brace. Milky oats (Avena sativa), harvested from the green stalks of the oat plant, act as a tonic for the whole nervous system - kindling resilience while calming frayed edges, like a warm hand on your neck at the end of a hard day.

You steep these herbs in hot water, watching the amber liquid swirl, inhaling the faint grassy scent that promises relief. As you drink, you feel the first slow thaw in your shoulders, the subtle exhale of trapped tension. This is not magic or quick fix; this is an ongoing conversation with your body, an acknowledgment that the cost of boundary-setting is not just emotional but deeply physical. Pema Chödrön’s words echo softly: “Allow yourself to sit with the discomfort, to feel your edges, to open your heart to what is.”

At this six-month mark, your body is still learning how to carry your no without collapsing under the weight of their yeses you refused. The trapezius and levator scapulae are messengers of this ongoing negotiation, their tension a call to care - not just for others, but for yourself. The herbs remind you that you are not alone in this. The body knows how to heal, if you give it the space, the breath, and the medicine it needs.

So tonight, as you cradle that warm cup, feel the slow release in your neck, the softening of muscles that have carried so much. This is your reckoning and your remedy, woven into the fibers of your being, six months after you spoke up and began to carry your own weight.

## The Nine-Month Mark: The Grief That Hides Under the Anger

Nine months out from the moment you finally spoke up  -  that precise, seismic instant you let your voice crack like thunder through the tight corridors of your silence  -  the body is still unraveling what you set loose. You think you might have been done with it, that the rage that once roiled your ribcage like a storm has settled into something quieter, more manageable. But the body remembers, and it knows better. Beneath the raw, hot flare of anger lies a different, older current, something quieter but no less fierce: grief.

You might notice it first as a subtle softening in your diaphragm, that dome of muscle sitting just under your lungs, the one that usually clenches so tightly when you’re braced for fight or flight. It’s the diaphragm that carries the weight of your breath, and when grief begins to release, it feels like a slow, almost imperceptible sigh escaping the tissues where your anger once clenched. Maybe it happens in the early morning hours, just before dawn, when the world is still heavy with night and your body is finally allowed to exhale. The fascia around the diaphragm, the connective webbing that stubbornly holds your ribs and sternum in a cage, starts to loosen - not all at once, but in small, subtle waves. It might feel like a soft ache in the solar plexus, a tender hollowing that wasn’t there before.

This is not a failure or a sign that your anger was misplaced. It’s the biology of survival and healing unfolding in real time. Your diaphragm remembers the long years you held your breath, the silent screaming beneath your skin. The grief that was smothered by fury now whispers its own story through the tissues and the breath. It is the body’s way of telling you that beneath the rage is something tender, vulnerable, and deeply human.

Gabor Maté, whose work on trauma and suppressed emotion I return to often, reminds us that the cost of holding in our feelings is a high one  -  sometimes higher than the cost of expression. He points out that when we suppress anger, we are also suppressing the grief that underpins it. The chest tightens, the breath shallows, and the diaphragm locks down like a clenched fist. The body becomes a fortress, but it’s a fortress built on the foundation of uncried tears and unspoken loss.

When you finally spoke up, you paid a price, for sure. There were risks, pushback, maybe even the sting of alienation. Yet Maté’s perspective shifts your focus: the cost of expression, while real, often pales beside the chronic toll of suppression. That chronic toll is written in the tension of your trapezius muscle, the shallow rise and fall of your breath, the tightness in your levator scapulae that never quite lets you rest your head. It’s in the gut where the psoas muscle holds the memory of every time you swallowed your truth.

Now, at nine months, the body is beginning to trust the space you’ve carved out. The diaphragm is learning to move freely again, expanding and contracting with a rhythm that isn’t dictated by fear or the need to brace against the world. The grief that rises is not a sign of weakness; it’s the body’s way of mourning the years you spent in hiding, the times you betrayed yourself to keep the peace. It’s a necessary shedding, a somatic clearing of the debris left behind by the storm of your anger.

You might find yourself weeping unexpectedly, the tears hot and fast, tasting of salt and old sorrow. Let them come. This is the body’s way of washing the internal landscape, of softening the hardened edges of your fascia. The grief is the water that nourishes the new growth pushing up through the scorched earth of your anger. It’s the quiet, persistent rain that follows the lightning strike.

And as the diaphragm releases, as the breath deepens and the chest opens, you might notice a subtle shift in how you carry yourself. The shoulders drop a fraction of an inch. The jaw unclenches just a little more easily. The body is settling into its new shape, a shape that is no longer defined by what it’s holding back, but by what it’s finally allowing to flow through. This is the deep, somatic work of healing, the slow, messy, beautiful process of becoming whole again.

## The One-Year Mark: The New Normal

A year has passed since the words finally broke free from your throat. The sharp, jagged edges of that initial rupture have smoothed out, worn down by the slow, steady rhythm of days turning into months. The body, once a battlefield of clenched muscles and spiked adrenaline, is beginning to settle into a new shape. It’s not the shape you had before - that old container was too small, too rigid, built to hold a silence that was suffocating you. This new shape is wider, deeper, and infinitely more resilient.

You notice it first in the fascia, that intricate web of connective tissue that wraps around every muscle, bone, and organ. For so long, it was tight, bound up in the effort of holding your truth at bay. It felt like wearing a suit of armor that was two sizes too small. But now, the fascia is unwinding. It’s a subtle sensation, like a slow thaw after a long, bitter winter. You might feel it as a gentle release in the hips, a softening in the belly, or a sudden, unexpected ease in the way your ribs expand when you take a deep breath. The body is learning that it no longer has to brace for impact. It is learning to trust the space you’ve claimed for yourself.

This unwinding is not a linear process. There are days when the old tension creeps back in, when the masseter muscle tightens at a perceived slight, or the psoas grips in response to a sudden stressor. But the baseline has shifted. The body’s default state is no longer one of chronic hypervigilance. It is finding a new equilibrium, a new normal that is grounded in the reality of your expressed truth.

To support this ongoing unwinding, we turn to a simple, embodied practice. This is not about achieving a state of perfect calm; it’s about meeting the body where it is and offering it a signal of safety.

**A Practice for Grounding the Nervous System:**

Find a quiet space where you won’t be disturbed. Sit or lie down in a comfortable position. Close your eyes if that feels safe, or let your gaze soften and rest on a point in front of you.

Bring your attention to the base of your spine, where the pelvis meets the earth or the chair. Feel the weight of your body settling into that support. Notice the sensation of gravity holding you, anchoring you in this moment.

Now, bring your awareness to your breath. Don’t try to change it; just observe the natural rhythm of the inhale and the exhale. Notice where the breath moves in your body. Does it fill the belly? Does it expand the ribs? Does it lift the collarbones?

As you breathe, imagine a warm, heavy blanket draped over your shoulders. Feel the weight of it pressing down gently, encouraging the trapezius and levator scapulae to release their grip. With each exhale, imagine the tension melting away, flowing down your arms and out through your fingertips.

Next, bring your attention to your jaw. Notice if the masseter muscle is clenched. If it is, gently part your lips and let the lower jaw drop slightly. Feel the space between your upper and lower teeth. Imagine the tension in your jaw dissolving, like sugar melting in warm water.

Finally, bring your awareness to your belly. Notice if the muscles there are tight or holding. With each inhale, allow the belly to soften and expand. With each exhale, let it release and sink back toward the spine. Imagine the fascia in your belly unwinding, creating space for your breath, your emotions, and your truth.

Stay with this practice for a few minutes, or as long as feels supportive. When you’re ready, gently open your eyes and take a moment to notice how your body feels. This is your new normal - a body that is learning to hold its own truth with grace and resilience.

## The Subtle Shifts: Adaptogens and the Nervous System

As the year unfolds, the dramatic peaks and valleys of your nervous system’s response begin to level out. The initial shockwaves of speaking up - the adrenaline spikes, the cortisol crashes, the somatic bracing - have subsided, leaving behind a quieter, more persistent hum of recalibration. This is the phase where the subtle shifts happen, the deep, cellular work of rebuilding a nervous system that has been fundamentally altered by the act of claiming your voice.

You might notice that your energy levels are different now. The frantic, wired energy that fueled your initial boundary-setting has given way to a more grounded, sustainable vitality. But there are still moments of fatigue, a bone-deep weariness that speaks to the immense energetic cost of the past year. The adrenal glands, those small, walnut-shaped organs sitting atop your kidneys, have been working overtime, pumping out stress hormones to keep you afloat. Now, they need support. They need nourishment.

This is where adaptogenic herbs come in. These are not quick fixes or superficial stimulants; they are deep, systemic tonics that help the body adapt to stress and restore balance. They work by modulating the body’s stress response, supporting the adrenal glands, and nourishing the nervous system at a foundational level.

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) is a powerful ally in this phase. Its name translates to "smell of the horse," a nod to its grounding, earthy aroma and its traditional use for imparting the strength and vitality of a stallion. But its true magic lies in its ability to soothe a frayed nervous system while simultaneously building resilience. It’s like a warm, heavy blanket for the soul, calming the mind and easing the body into a state of deep, restorative rest. You might take it as a tincture, its dark, earthy taste a reminder of the deep roots you are putting down, or as a powder mixed into warm milk with a touch of honey and cardamom.

Holy Basil (Ocimum sanctum), also known as Tulsi, is another profound adaptogen. In Ayurvedic medicine, it is revered as a sacred plant, a "liquid yoga" that brings clarity, calm, and a sense of spiritual uplift. But its effects are deeply somatic. It helps to clear the mental fog that often accompanies chronic stress, while gently supporting the adrenal glands and easing tension in the chest and diaphragm. A cup of strong Tulsi tea, its aromatic, slightly spicy scent filling the air, can be a daily ritual of recalibration, a moment to pause and let the body remember its own inherent wisdom.

These herbs are not just supplements; they are partners in your healing journey. They remind the body that it is supported, that it doesn’t have to carry the weight of the world alone. As you integrate these adaptogens into your daily routine, you might notice a subtle but profound shift in how you respond to stress. The startle response softens. The jaw unclenches a little more easily. The breath deepens. The body is learning a new language, a language of resilience, adaptability, and deep, somatic trust.

This is the quiet work of the year after you spoke up. It’s the slow, steady process of tending to the soil of your nervous system, nourishing the roots so that the new growth can thrive. It’s a testament to the body’s incredible capacity for healing, and a reminder that the journey of claiming your voice is not just about the words you say, but about the body you inhabit.

## FAQ: The Physical and Relational Toll of Speaking Up

**Q: Why does my body still feel so exhausted months after I finally set a boundary?**

**A:** Because setting a boundary isn’t just a mental exercise; it’s a full-body event. When you spoke up, your nervous system went into overdrive, pumping out adrenaline and cortisol to give you the courage to say the words. That takes a massive toll on your adrenal glands and your deep tissues. The exhaustion you’re feeling is the body’s way of demanding repair. The psoas, the masseter, the diaphragm - they’ve all been bracing for impact. Now that the immediate threat has passed, the body is finally allowing itself to collapse. This is not pathology, this is biology. Rest is not a luxury right now; it’s a biological imperative.

**Q: I thought speaking my truth would make me feel free, but instead, I feel a deep sense of grief. Is this normal?**

**A:** Absolutely. When you stop using your energy to suppress your anger and hold your boundaries in place, the emotions that were hiding underneath finally have room to breathe. Often, that emotion is grief. You’re mourning the years you spent silencing yourself, the relationships that couldn’t survive your truth, and the version of yourself you had to be to keep the peace. The diaphragm releases, the chest opens, and the tears come. Let them. This grief is the water that nourishes the new growth pushing up through the scorched earth of your anger.

**Q: My relationships have shifted drastically since I spoke up. Some people have pulled away, and others seem angry with me. How do I handle this?**

**A:** When you change the rules of engagement, the people who benefited from your silence are going to be uncomfortable. Karla McLaren reminds us that anger is a boundary-restoring force, but boundaries unsettle people. The withdrawal or retaliation you’re experiencing is their nervous system reacting to the change. You cannot control their response; you can only tend to your own body. Notice where you’re holding their disappointment - is it in your trapezius? Your levator scapulae? Use herbs like skullcap or milky oats to support your nervous system, and remember that their reaction is information, not an indictment of your truth.

**Q: What herbs can I use to support my body during this transition?**

**A:** In the immediate aftermath, when the nervous system is frayed and the muscles are tight, nervines are your best friends. Skullcap and milky oats are excellent for soothing the nervous system and easing muscle tension. As you move into the later months and the focus shifts to rebuilding resilience, adaptogens like Ashwagandha and Holy Basil (Tulsi) are invaluable. They support the adrenal glands, modulate the stress response, and help the body find a new, grounded baseline. Remember, these herbs are serious medicine. Treat them with respect and consistency.

**Q: Will my body ever feel "normal" again?**

**A:** It won’t feel like the old normal, because that normal was built on suppression and silence. Your body is finding a new normal, one that is wider, deeper, and more resilient. The fascia is unwinding, the muscles are learning to release their chronic grip, and the nervous system is recalibrating. It takes time - often a full year or more - for the body to fully integrate the somatic shift of speaking up. Be patient with the process. The body knows how to heal, if you give it the space, the breath, and the medicine it needs.

In our work on the year after you spoke up, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
</section>
